blocks.statement: I can explain why extending Δt reduces the force needed to deliver the same Δp, and I can apply this to airbags, padded floors, follow-through, and asteroid deflection.

blocks.whatWeKnow: To deflect 2026-XJ we want the LARGEST Δp possible — either short-and-hard (DART) or long-and-soft (gravity tractor).
blocks.connection: Same physics that saves lives in a car crash (long Δt, small F) shapes how planetary defense engineers design asteroid pushers.

blocks.markdown:
## The trade nobody can escape

J = F·Δt is a fixed budget: if the Δp is decided (a sprinter must stop; a ball must reverse), then **F and Δt trade off exactly**.

- Stretch Δt by 10× → F drops to **1/10**.
- That's an airbag, a crumple zone, a gymnastics mat, a parkour roll, bent knees on a landing.
- Flip the goal: a hitter wants MORE Δp delivered to the ball. **Follow-through** extends Δt → bigger J → bigger Δp → ball goes farther.

Same equation, both directions.

terms.definition: A region designed to DEFORM during impact, extending Δt and so reducing peak force. Modern car fronts are designed to crumple: same Δp, but Δt × 5 means F ÷ 5.
terms.cognate: Sp. zona de absorción · Pt. zona de deformação

blocks.title: A crumple zone doesn't 'absorb momentum'
blocks.markdown: Δp is the SAME either way — the car still stops. The crumple zone **spreads the time**, so the peak force drops. Don't say momentum was absorbed; say the time was stretched.

blocks.title: Same Δp = 720 kg·m/s — force vs. stopping time
blocks.genPrompt: F = 720/Δt hyperbola showing the force-time tradeoff for a fixed impulse of 720 N·s.

blocks.prompt: An 80-kg sprinter at 9 m/s stops in (a) 0.05 s on concrete, or (b) 0.5 s into a foam pit. Compute the average force in each case.
blocks.given: m = 80 kg · Δv = 9 m/s (he stops) · Δt_a = 0.05 s · Δt_b = 0.5 s
blocks.work:
Δp = 80·9 = 720 kg·m/s (same for both)
F_a = 720 / 0.05 = 14,400 N (concrete)
F_b = 720 / 0.5 = 1,440 N (foam — 10× smaller!)
blocks.answer: Concrete: 14.4 kN. Foam: 1.44 kN. Stretching Δt by 10× cut F by 10×.

blocks.prompt: Pick one: airbag, helmet, boxing glove, gymnastics mat, parkour roll, or follow-through on a tennis swing. Explain how it uses LONGER Δt to reduce F for the SAME Δp (or, for follow-through, longer Δt to deliver MORE Δp). Be specific about what physically extends the contact time.
blocks.givenHint: Name your case and what the Δp is (what stops, or what speeds up).
blocks.equationHint: J = F·Δt = Δp — argue with the equation, not just words.

blocks.prompt: Why does a boxer 'roll with the punch'? Use 'Δp', 'Δt', and 'F' in your answer.
blocks.frame: Rolling with the punch keeps ___ roughly the same but makes ___ larger — which makes ___ smaller.
